source.title: Precursors to morality in development as a complex interplay between neural, socioenvironmental, and behavioral facets
source.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

confidence_rationale: Multi-method study (EEG, ERP, gaze, socioenvironmental) across 73 infants/toddlers demonstrates that moral sensitivity is not a simple additive overlay but emerges through staged integration of bottom-up emotional and top-down evaluative processes, with parental-value modulation. This supports the developmental-not-additive thesis of Q-006. The main transfer risk is the gap between infant moral perception and adult ethical agency.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Neural differentiation between prosocial and antisocial agents emerges by 12-24 months and is modulated by parental values; early automatic emotional responses precede and scaffold later cognitive reappraisal, forming a staged developmental progression.
mapping.ree_translation: Ethical sensitivity in REE cannot be installed as a discrete module at a late training stage -- the architecture must be trained through exposure sequences that progressively build emotional-evaluative integration, mirroring the staged neural scaffolding described here. ARC-015's insistence on ground-up ethical capacity is consistent with this.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The study measures moral perception (evaluation of third-party agents) in pre-linguistic infants, not moral agency or decision-making in adults. REE requires trained agency, not merely perceptual discrimination. The claimed developmental necessity may reflect a human-specific neural timetable with no direct analogue in gradient-based learning systems.
mapping.source_context: Human infants and toddlers aged 12-24 months, EEG/ERP and eye-tracking, helper/hinderer social evaluation task.

confidence_components.notes: High-quality PNAS empirical study with multi-level measurement. Mapping fidelity is moderate because the developmental necessity claim in biological organisms does not map directly to artificial training schedules. Transfer risk is substantial: biological neural scaffolding over months is architecturally very different from gradient descent over episodes.
